did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Support Vector Machine (SVM) machine learning algorithm to classify skin samples as normal or melanin-rich based on Raman spectroscopy peak intensities, achieving an accuracy of 88.9%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work, including the construction of a dual-modality optical biopsy system, creation of melanin-treated skin models, Raman spectroscopy and OCT measurements, and machine learning classification results (88.9% accuracy).

title: Detection of melanin influence on skin samples based on Raman spectroscopy and optical coherence tomography dual-modal approach
brief_summary: This study developed a dual-modality optical biopsy system combining Raman spectroscopy and optical coherence tomography (OCT) to noninvasively detect the influence of melanin on skin samples. Fresh porcine skin samples were treated with synthetic melanin dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to mimic melanoma and darker skin types. The system utilized OCT for morphological imaging and precise localization of melanin-rich regions, while Raman spectroscopy analyzed molecular changes, revealing decreased intensities in amide-I and CH2-deformation bands. A Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm was employed to classify the skin samples as normal or melanin-rich based on characteristic Raman peak intensities. The machine learning model achieved an accuracy of 88.9% in distinguishing between normal and melanin-treated skin using 10-fold cross-validation. The results demonstrate the potential of this dual-modal approach for noninvasive melanoma diagnostics by effectively capturing both morphological and biochemical alterations induced by melanin.

paper_terms: samples injected with the M-DMSO solution
samples immersed in the M-DMSO solution
lesion_types_studied.details: The study did not use actual melanoma tissue samples. Instead, it used fresh porcine skin samples treated with synthetic melanin dissolved in DMSO to mimic melanoma and evaluate the effect of melanin on skin. These treated samples served as the positive class for the classification task.
Untreated fresh porcine skin samples served as the negative class (normal baseline) for comparison against melanin-treated samples.

Mentioned in the introduction and discussion sections when citing previous studies (e.g., Schleusener et al., Huang et al.) regarding Raman spectroscopy and melanoma diagnosis. Not used in the current study's experiments.

use.validation: Used as input for the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classification model (negative class) via 10-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: Melanin-Treated Porcine Skin Samples
datasets_used.description: Fresh porcine skin samples treated with synthetic melanin dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to mimic melanoma and darker skin types. Treatments included immersion in M-DMSO solution for 30 minutes, 1 day, and 2 days, as well as injection with M-DMSO solution.

use.training: Used as input for the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classification model (positive class) via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used as input for the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classification model (positive class) via 10-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To reduce the dimensionality of spectral data for visualization and to observe trends/distributions of different skin samples based on Chebyshev distance metrics.

statistical_methods_used.method: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Bayesian optimization
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To classify different skin types (normal vs. melanin-rich) based on Raman peak intensities, achieving an accuracy of 88.9%.
To evaluate the performance of the SVM classification model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported descriptive statistics (median normalized intensities and ratios) and machine learning classification performance (accuracy, true positive rate) without statistical significance testing.

Support Vector Machine (SVM).notes: Served as the baseline class for classification. Specific peak intensities: 1120 cm-1: 1.24, 1320 cm-1: 2.56, 1450 cm-1: 2.32, 1650 cm-1: 4.35. Ratio I1120/1650: 0.28, I1320/1650: 0.60, I1450/1650: 0.53.

Fresh Porcine Skin (Normal).notes: OCT imaging depth: 250 μm. Signal intensity decreased more slowly at the beginning and then gradually faster compared to treated samples. At depths >300 μm, signal intensity was 1–3 dB lower than immersed samples.

Melanin-Treated Porcine Skin (M-DMSO Imm.notes: Spectral distribution close to fresh skin. Peak intensities: 1120 cm-1: 1.26, 1320 cm-1: 2.72, 1450 cm-1: 2.29, 1650 cm-1: 4.31. Ratio I1120/1650: 0.30, I1320/1650: 0.63, I1450/1650: 0.53.
Spectral distribution moved away from fresh samples. Peak intensities: 1120 cm-1: 2.23, 1320 cm-1: 4.19, 1450 cm-1: 1.68, 1650 cm-1: 3.80. Ratio I1120/1650: 0.43, I1320/1650: 0.93, I1450/1650: 0.46.

Melanin-Treated Porcine Skin (M-DMSO Inj.notes: Highly concentrated distribution in t-SNE, separated from other samples. Peak intensities: 1120 cm-1: 2.29, 1320 cm-1: 2.91, 1450 cm-1: 1.64, 1650 cm-1: 3.81. Ratio I1120/1650: 0.46, I1320/1650: 0.76, I1450/1650: 0.42.

Melanin-Treated Porcine Skin (DMSO Immer.notes: Distributions very close for different immersion times (30 min, 1 day, 2 days), indicating DMSO effect is distinct from melanin effect. Samples immersed for 2 days mixed with M-DMSO data.

architecture_used.input_size: Characteristic Raman peak intensities (specifically amide-I, CH2-twist, C–C stretch, and CH2-deformation bands) and relative ratios (I1120/1650, I1320/1650, I1450/1650) derived from Raman spectra.

architecture_details.input_features: Median normalized intensities of typical lipid and protein peaks (1120 cm-1, 1320 cm-1, 1450 cm-1, 1650 cm-1) and their ratios relative to the amide-I band (1650 cm-1).

training_details.hyperparameters: Optimized via Bayesian optimization (specific kernel or C/gamma values not reported)

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Binary classification (Normal skin vs. Melanin-rich skin)

preprocessing_techniques: Mollifier-based baseline correction to remove background interference from fluorescence and obtain targeted spectra
Normalization of spectral peak intensities using the amide-I band (1650 cm⁻¹) as a reference to calculate relative ratios
Averaging of OCT A-scan results along the B-scan direction and across all samples in a group to analyze signal intensity decline
Averaging of Raman spectral results for all samples within the same group
conclusions: A dual-modality optical biopsy system combining Raman spectroscopy and optical coherence tomography (OCT) was successfully developed for noninvasive skin measurement.
Synthetic melanin dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was used to create ex vivo porcine skin models that mimic melanoma and darker skin types.
Melanin treatment increased the OCT imaging depth from 250 μm to 350 μm due to the optical clearing effect of the DMSO solvent.

Raman spectroscopy analysis revealed that relative spectral intensities in melanin-treated samples were lower in the amide-I and CH2-deformation bands and higher in the CH2-twist and C–C stretch bands compared to untreated samples.
The decrease in the CH2-deformation band relative to the amide-I band suggests that lipids are more affected by melanin than proteins.

A Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm using Bayesian optimization achieved an accuracy of 88.9% (reported as 89% in the abstract) in classifying normal versus melanin-treated skin based on Raman peak intensities.
The study demonstrates that Raman spectroscopy can serve as a basis for the reliable identification of melanoma.

The authors acknowledge that the current classification model only used Raman spectral characteristics and that future work will incorporate OCT parameters (such as signal drop and penetration depth) to further improve classification accuracy.
The authors state that the ability of these signatures to discriminate melanoma needs to be further demonstrated by in vivo measurements on melanoma skin cancer patients in a clinical setting.
important_remarks: The study uses ex vivo fresh porcine skin treated with synthetic melanin and DMSO to mimic melanoma, rather than actual human melanoma tissue or in vivo clinical samples, limiting direct clinical applicability.

The classification model (SVM) relies exclusively on Raman spectroscopy peak intensities; OCT parameters (e.g., signal drop, penetration depth) were used only for localization and morphological analysis, not for the machine learning classification, representing a suboptimal use of the dual-modal system.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) for the spectral differences or classification performance, relying instead on descriptive statistics and cross-validation accuracy.

The specific sample sizes (n) for each treatment group (fresh, 30 min immersion, 1 day immersion, injection) are not explicitly stated in the provided text, preventing assessment of statistical power.

The use of DMSO as a solvent introduces an optical clearing effect that increases OCT imaging depth (250 μm to 350 μm), which may confound the interpretation of melanin-specific morphological changes.
The model achieved 88.9% accuracy using 10-fold cross-validation, but external validation on independent datasets or human subjects was not performed.
The study acknowledges that the ability of these signatures to discriminate melanoma needs further demonstration in a clinical setting with in vivo measurements on melanoma skin cancer patients.
